questions.question_text: A two-way frequency table shows that 30 students play soccer, 20 play basketball, 10 play both, and 15 play neither. How many students were surveyed in total?
questions.explanation: Using the inclusion-exclusion principle: total = soccer + basketball - both + neither = 30 + 20 - 10 + 15 = 55 students.

questions.question_text: In a two-way table, the row totals are 45 and 55. What is the grand total?
questions.explanation: The grand total is the sum of all row totals: 45 + 55 = 100.

questions.question_text: A two-way table shows: Male & Cat = 12, Male & Dog = 18, Female & Cat = 15, Female & Dog = 25. What is the total number of dog owners?
questions.explanation: Total dog owners = Male & Dog + Female & Dog = 18 + 25 = 43.

questions.question_text: Using the table: Male & Cat = 12, Male & Dog = 18, Female & Cat = 15, Female & Dog = 25. How many females were surveyed?
questions.explanation: Total females = Female & Cat + Female & Dog = 15 + 25 = 40.

questions.question_text: A two-way table has a grand total of 200. The row for 'Yes' has a total of 80. How many people answered 'No'?
questions.explanation: People who answered 'No' = grand total - 'Yes' total = 200 - 80 = 120.

questions.question_text: In a two-way table, the column totals are 60 and 90. What is the grand total?
questions.explanation: The grand total is the sum of all column totals: 60 + 90 = 150.

questions.question_text: A table shows: Freshman & Bus = 40, Freshman & Car = 20, Senior & Bus = 25, Senior & Car = 35. What fraction of all students ride the bus?
questions.explanation: Total bus riders = 40 + 25 = 65. Grand total = 40 + 20 + 25 + 35 = 120. The fraction is 65/120.

questions.question_text: A two-way table has cells A = 10, B = 20, C = 30, D = 40. What percentage of the total does cell C represent?
questions.explanation: Grand total = 10 + 20 + 30 + 40 = 100. Cell C as a percentage = (30/100) x 100% = 30%.

questions.question_text: A survey of 80 people asks about pet preference and living situation. If 50 live in houses and 30 live in apartments, and 35 prefer dogs, how many apartment dwellers could prefer cats if 20 house dwellers prefer dogs?
questions.explanation: Dog owners in apartments = 35 - 20 = 15. Cat owners total = 80 - 35 = 45. Cat owners in houses = 50 - 20 = 30. Cat owners in apartments = 45 - 30 = 15.

questions.question_text: A two-way table shows the results of a survey with 2 rows and 2 columns. How many cells (not including totals) does this table have?
questions.explanation: A table with 2 rows and 2 columns has 2 x 2 = 4 data cells (not counting the row totals, column totals, or grand total).

questions.question_text: What is a two-way frequency table?

options.text: A table that displays the frequency of one categorical variable
A table that displays frequencies for two categorical variables, with rows for one variable and columns for the other
A table that shows only percentages
A scatter plot in table form

questions.explanation: A two-way frequency table organizes data for two categorical variables simultaneously, using rows for one variable and columns for the other, with counts in each cell.

questions.question_text: What is a 'joint frequency' in a two-way table?

options.text: The total of an entire row or column
The count in an individual cell representing the intersection of two categories
The grand total of the table
The average of all cells

questions.explanation: A joint frequency is the count in a single cell of the table, representing how many data points fall into the intersection of a specific row category and a specific column category.

questions.question_text: What is a 'marginal frequency'?

options.text: The count in an individual interior cell of the table
The sum of the frequencies in a row or column, typically found in the margins of the table
The difference between two cell values
The grand total divided by the number of cells

questions.explanation: A marginal frequency is the total of a row or column, found along the margins (edges) of the two-way table. It summarizes data for one category across all categories of the other variable.

questions.question_text: What does 'categorical variable' mean?

options.text: A variable that takes numerical values that can be averaged
A variable that represents groups or categories, such as gender, color, or type
A variable that is always continuous
A variable that can only take the value 0 or 1

questions.explanation: A categorical variable represents groups or categories rather than numerical quantities. Examples include gender, favorite color, and type of transportation.

questions.question_text: What does 'frequency' mean in a frequency table?

options.text: The percentage of data in a category
The number of times a particular value or category occurs in a data set
The average of a data set
The range of the data

questions.explanation: Frequency is the count of how many times a specific value, response, or category appears in the data set.

questions.question_text: What is the 'grand total' in a two-way frequency table?

options.text: The largest single cell value in the table
The total number of observations represented in the entire table
The sum of only the first row
The average of all marginal frequencies

questions.explanation: The grand total is the total count of all observations in the table. It equals the sum of all row totals or equivalently the sum of all column totals.

questions.question_text: What does 'conditional frequency' mean?

options.text: The total of all cells in the table
The frequency of one category given that a specific condition or category of another variable is met
The average of two marginal frequencies
The count in the largest cell

questions.explanation: A conditional frequency is the frequency of one variable calculated within a specific category of another variable, such as the number of males who prefer cats out of all males.

questions.question_text: What does the term 'row variable' mean in a two-way table?

options.text: The variable whose categories label the columns
The variable whose categories label the rows of the table
The grand total row
A variable that is always numerical

questions.explanation: The row variable is the categorical variable whose different categories are listed along the rows of the two-way table.

questions.question_text: What does 'survey data' refer to in the context of two-way tables?

options.text: Data collected only through laboratory experiments
Data collected by asking individuals questions about their preferences, behaviors, or characteristics
Data that is always numerical
Data presented exclusively in scatter plots

questions.explanation: Survey data is information gathered by questioning individuals about their preferences, behaviors, or characteristics. This data is commonly organized into two-way frequency tables.

questions.question_text: What does 'cell' mean in the context of a two-way frequency table?

options.text: The title of the table
An individual box at the intersection of a row and column that contains a frequency count
The row or column total
A type of graph

questions.explanation: A cell is the individual box located at the intersection of a specific row and column in the table, containing the count (frequency) of observations that belong to both categories.

questions.question_text: A school surveys 150 students about their lunch preference (pizza or salad) and grade level (9th or 10th). The results are: 9th & Pizza = 40, 9th & Salad = 35, 10th & Pizza = 45, 10th & Salad = 30. What percentage of all students prefer pizza?

options.text: About 56.7%
About 43.3%
About 50%
About 60%

questions.explanation: Total pizza = 40 + 45 = 85. Percentage = (85/150) x 100% = 56.67%, which is approximately 56.7%.

questions.question_text: A company surveys 200 employees about commute type (drive or public transit) and department (Sales or Engineering). Results: Sales & Drive = 50, Sales & Transit = 30, Engineering & Drive = 60, Engineering & Transit = 60. What fraction of Engineering employees use public transit?
questions.explanation: Total Engineering = 60 + 60 = 120. Engineering using transit = 60. Fraction = 60/120 = 1/2.

questions.question_text: A veterinary clinic records the type of pet (cat or dog) and whether the pet is vaccinated (yes or no). Results: Cat & Yes = 28, Cat & No = 12, Dog & Yes = 35, Dog & No = 25. If a pet is randomly selected, what is the probability it is a vaccinated dog?
questions.explanation: Grand total = 28 + 12 + 35 + 25 = 100. Vaccinated dogs = 35. Probability = 35/100 = 0.35.

questions.question_text: A college admissions office tracks acceptance status (accepted or rejected) by residency (in-state or out-of-state). Results: In-state & Accepted = 120, In-state & Rejected = 80, Out-of-state & Accepted = 60, Out-of-state & Rejected = 140. What percentage of out-of-state applicants were accepted?
questions.explanation: Total out-of-state = 60 + 140 = 200. Out-of-state accepted = 60. Percentage = (60/200) x 100% = 30%.

questions.question_text: A gym surveys 180 members about workout preference (cardio or weights) and membership type (monthly or annual). Results: Monthly & Cardio = 35, Monthly & Weights = 45, Annual & Cardio = 50, Annual & Weights = 50. Is there a difference in workout preference between monthly and annual members?

options.text: Yes, monthly members prefer weights (56.25%) more than annual members (50%)
No, both groups have identical preferences
Yes, annual members strongly prefer cardio over weights
There is not enough data to determine

questions.explanation: Monthly: 45/80 = 56.25% prefer weights. Annual: 50/100 = 50% prefer weights. There is a slight difference: monthly members lean more toward weights.

questions.question_text: A hospital records patient outcomes (improved or no change) and treatment type (Drug A or Drug B). Results: Drug A & Improved = 70, Drug A & No Change = 30, Drug B & Improved = 55, Drug B & No Change = 45. Which drug had a higher rate of improvement?

options.text: Drug B, with 55% improvement
Drug A, with 70% improvement
Both drugs had equal improvement rates
Drug B, with 70% improvement

questions.explanation: Drug A: 70/100 = 70% improved. Drug B: 55/100 = 55% improved. Drug A had the higher improvement rate.

questions.question_text: A restaurant surveys 250 customers about meal preference (chicken or fish) and dining time (lunch or dinner). Results: Lunch & Chicken = 60, Lunch & Fish = 40, Dinner & Chicken = 80, Dinner & Fish = 70. What percentage of lunch customers chose fish?
questions.explanation: Total lunch = 60 + 40 = 100. Fish at lunch = 40. Percentage = (40/100) x 100% = 40%.

questions.question_text: A school collects data on student gender (male or female) and participation in extracurriculars (yes or no). Results: Male & Yes = 55, Male & No = 45, Female & Yes = 70, Female & No = 30. Which group has a higher participation rate?

options.text: Males, with 55% participation
Females, with 70% participation
Both groups have equal rates
Males, with 70% participation

questions.explanation: Males: 55/100 = 55%. Females: 70/100 = 70%. Females have a higher extracurricular participation rate.

questions.question_text: A phone store records sales by phone type (Android or iPhone) and payment method (cash or financing). Results: Android & Cash = 20, Android & Financing = 40, iPhone & Cash = 15, iPhone & Financing = 25. What percentage of all phone sales used financing?
questions.explanation: Total financing = 40 + 25 = 65. Grand total = 20 + 40 + 15 + 25 = 100. Percentage = (65/100) x 100% = 65%.

questions.question_text: A library surveys 300 patrons about reading preference (fiction or nonfiction) and age group (under 30 or 30 and over). Results: Under 30 & Fiction = 80, Under 30 & Nonfiction = 40, 30+ & Fiction = 90, 30+ & Nonfiction = 90. A librarian claims that younger patrons prefer fiction more strongly than older patrons. Do the data support this?

options.text: Yes; under 30 choose fiction at about 66.7% while 30+ choose fiction at 50%
No; both age groups prefer fiction equally
No; older patrons prefer fiction more than younger patrons
The data is inconclusive

questions.explanation: Under 30: 80/120 = 66.7% fiction. 30+: 90/180 = 50% fiction. The data support the claim: younger patrons prefer fiction at a notably higher rate.
